title: Rate Limiting
summary: Token bucket, sliding window, fixed window, leaky bucket algorithms, and distributed rate limiting with Redis.

sections.content:
## Why Rate Limit?

Rate limiting protects your API from:
- **DDoS attacks** — prevent traffic floods
- **Abuse** — stop one user consuming all resources
- **Cost control** — cap expensive operations per user
- **Fair usage** — ensure fair access for all users

## Rate Limiting Algorithms

### Fixed Window Counter
- Count requests per time window (e.g. 100 req/min)
- Reset counter at window boundary
- ✗ **Edge spike problem**: 100 req at 0:59 + 100 req at 1:01 = 200 req in 2 seconds!

### Sliding Window Log
- Track timestamp of each request in a log
- Count requests in the last N seconds
- ✓ Accurate, no edge spike
- ✗ Memory intensive (stores every timestamp)

### Token Bucket
- Bucket holds tokens (capacity = burst limit)
- Tokens added at fixed rate (refill rate = sustained limit)
- Each request consumes 1 token
- ✓ Allows controlled bursting
- ✓ Most common algorithm

### Leaky Bucket
- Requests enter a queue (bucket)
- Processed at fixed rate (leak rate)
- Excess requests dropped or queued
- ✓ Smooths out traffic
- ✗ No bursting allowed

sections.content:
graph LR

    Request[Incoming Request] -->|consume 1 token| Bucket
    Bucket -->|token available| Allow[✓ Allow Request]
    Bucket -->|empty| Deny[✗ 429 Too Many Requests]
    style Allow fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Deny fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Fixed Window Edge Spike Problem
sections.content:
graph LR
    W1[Window 1\n0:00 - 1:00\n100 requests allowed]
    W2[Window 2\n1:00 - 2:00\n100 requests allowed]
    Spike[100 requests at 0:59\n+ 100 requests at 1:01\n= 200 requests in 2 seconds!]
    W1 --> Spike
    W2 --> Spike
    style Spike fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

## Distributed Rate Limiting with Redis

Single-server rate limiting is simple. In a cluster, you need a **shared counter**.

### Redis Counter (Fixed Window)
```lua
-- Atomic Lua script runs in Redis
local key = KEYS[1]  -- e.g. "rate:user:123:minute:1234567"
local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local current = redis.call('INCR', key)
if current == 1 then
    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, 60)  -- set TTL on first request
end
if current > limit then
    return 0  -- denied
end
return 1  -- allowed
```

### Redis Sliding Window (sorted set)
```lua
local key = KEYS[1]
local now = tonumber(ARGV[1])  -- current timestamp ms
local window = tonumber(ARGV[2])  -- window size ms
local limit = tonumber(ARGV[3])

-- Remove old entries
redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, 0, now - window)

local count = redis.call('ZCARD', key)
if count >= limit then
    return 0  -- denied
end
redis.call('ZADD', key, now, now)  -- add current timestamp
redis.call('EXPIRE', key, window / 1000)
return 1  -- allowed
```

sections.question: What is the edge spike problem with fixed window rate limiting?
options: The counter resets too slowly causing requests to pile up
A user can send 2x the limit in a short period by sending max requests at the end of one window and the start of the next
Fixed windows don't work with distributed systems
The window size is too large for fast APIs
sections.explanation: With a 100 req/min fixed window, a user can send 100 requests at 0:59:59 (end of window 1) and 100 more at 1:00:01 (start of window 2). Both windows are within their limit, but the user sent 200 requests in 2 seconds. Sliding window algorithms prevent this by tracking requests over a rolling time window.
